coredata.dc:title: Enhanced selectivity of carbon quantum dots for metal ion detection through surface modification by heteroatom doping: A study on optical properties and theoretical approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cánchig, María Belén
López, Floralba
Morales-Navarro, Zaillmar
Debut, Alexis
Vizuete, Karla
Terencio, Thibault
Caetano, Manuel
Saucedo-Vázquez, Juan Pablo
coredata.dc:description: Water contamination by toxic metal ions has become a significant issue, requiring the development of effective ion detection methods. Traditional analytical techniques often involve toxic elements or complex devices. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising alternative for optic ion detection due to their unique properties and compatibility with living organisms. This study focuses on synthesizing and functionalizing CQDs with various heteroatoms (N, S) to enhance their optical properties and ion selectivity. CQDs were synthesized using citric acid as the carbon source and modified with l-cysteine, ethylenediamine, and diethylenetriamine. The structural and optical properties of the CQDs were determined using several techniques, including FT-IR, TEM, UV–Vis, and Fluorescence Spectroscopy. The results indicate that doping with heteroatoms significantly alters the absorption and emission properties of CQDs. Particularly, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) exhibited the highest absorption and emission intensities, making them ideal for sensor applications. The study also demonstrated that functionalization with sulfur could modulate emission frequencies, enhancing the detection capabilities for specific ions. Fluorescence quenching studies revealed that NCQDs and S-CQDs have a high selectivity for Hg²⁺ ions, attributed both electrostatic and covalent interactions formed between the CQDs and Hg²⁺. Computational studies supported these findings, showing that the interaction with Hg²⁺ significantly affects the energy gap of the CQDs, enhancing their sensitivity. This research contributes to the field of environmental monitoring by providing a practical solution for the detection of free metal ions in water through the development of advanced CQD-based sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Heteroatom-doped
Ion detection
Heavy metals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs with concentration
The geometry optimization of CQDs and CQDs with Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of citric acid (grey) used as precursor of CQDs, surface modifiers l-cysteine (a-green), ethylenediamine (b-green), and c) diethylenetriamine (c-green), and their respective functionalized CQDs: L-CQDs (a-blue), E-CQDs (b-blue) and D-CQDs (c-blue).
Micrographs obtained by TEM and size distribution histograms for L-CQDs (a, b), E-CQDs and (c, d) and D-CQDs (e, f).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of synthesized CQDs with a) l-cysteine b) Ethylenediamine and c) Diethylenetriamine.
PL spectra of a) L-CQDs, b) E-CQDs and c) D-CQDs at the excitation wavelengths of 365, 465, and 635 nm. Inset: fluorescence of each solution when are excited by UV light at 365 nm.
PL of different dilutions of L- CQDs, b) E-CQDs and c) D-CQDs. Inset: PL intensity as function of the concentration of CQDs.
The I/I0 ratio of L- CQDs, b) E-CQDs and c) D-CQDs in the presence of 50 ppm of different metal ions.
Quenching effect of Cd2+ over a) L- CQDs, and Hg2+ over b) E-CQDs and c) D-CQDs. Calibration curves for the detection of Cd2+ by d) L- CQDs, and Hg2+ by e) E-CQDs and f) D-CQDs.
The 4 CQDs models with different structural features a) Only Carbon (C) b) N-Modifier (NCQDs) c) O Modifier (OCQDs) and d) S-Modifier (S-CQDs) optimized by CAM B3LYP.
Optic properties of L-CQDs, E-CQDs, and D-CQDs.

HOMO-LUMO Energy Levels and Band Gaps for Different Quantum Dots Structures, with and without Hg 2+ Ions.
Interaction energy between Hg2+ Ions and each carbon dot model.

head.ce:title: Enhanced selectivity of carbon quantum dots for metal ion detection through surface modification by heteroatom doping: A study on optical properties and theoretical approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MEDCHEM Research Group, School of Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Yachay Tech University, Urcuquí, 100119, Ecuador

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MEDCHEM Research Group, School of Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Yachay Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMEDCHEM Research Group, School of Chemical Sciences Engineering, Yachay Tech University, Urcuquí 100119, Ecuador

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Nanociencia y Nanotecnología, Universidad de las Fuerzas Armadas ESPE, Sangolquí 171523, Ecuador

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Nanociencia y Nanotecnología, Universidad de las Fuerzas Armadas ESPE

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCentro de Nanociencia y Nanotecnología, Universidad de las Fuerzas Armadas ESPE, Sangolquí 171523, Ecuador

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contamination by toxic metal ions has become a significant issue, requiring the development of effective ion detection methods. Traditional analytical techniques often involve toxic elements or complex devices. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising alternative for optic ion detection due to their unique properties and compatibility with living organisms. This study focuses on synthesizing and functionalizing CQDs with various heteroatoms (N, S) to enhance their optical properties and ion selectivity. CQDs were synthesized using citric acid as the carbon source and modified with l-cysteine, ethylenediamine, and diethylenetriamine. The structural and optical properties of the CQDs were determined using several techniques, including FT-IR, TEM, UV–Vis, and Fluorescence Spectroscopy. The results indicate that doping with heteroatoms significantly alters the absorption and emission properties of CQDs. Particularly, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) exhibited the highest absorption and emission intensities, making them ideal for sensor applications. The study also demonstrated that functionalization with sulfur could modulate emission frequencies, enhancing the detection capabilities for specific ions. Fluorescence quenching studies revealed that NCQDs and S-CQDs have a high selectivity for Hg²⁺ ions, attributed both electrostatic and covalent interactions formed between the CQDs and Hg²⁺. Computational studies supported these findings, showing that the interaction with Hg²⁺ significantly affects the energy gap of the CQDs, enhancing their sensitivity. This research contributes to the field of environmental monitoring by providing a practical solution for the detection of free metal ions in water through the development of advanced CQD-based sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Functionalization of nanomaterials, involving the incorporation of chemical motifs, functional groups, or ligands onto their surface, alters their physical, chemical, or biological properties to confer specific desired characteristics for specific applications. It can be designed through the choice of functional groups to fine-tune surface properties for enhanced reactivity, selectivity, biocompatibility, or stability in many different environments [1]. Among nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant interest due to their distinctive optical properties, notably tunable luminescence, high quantum yield, and excellent biocompatibility [2]. These CQDs are typically spherical nanoparticles composed of carbon, with sizes generally below 10 nm. They exhibit remarkable fluorescent behavior that can be modified through various synthesis methods and surface functionalization [3–6]. Since their discovery, CQDs have been extensively studied due to their optical properties, and biocompatibility, making them suitable for a wide range of applications such as optical sensors, solar cells, drug delivery, bioimaging, and, notably, environmental sensing (Fig. 1 ) [7–11].

Over the past few years, the growing need for effective control and remediation of environmental pollutants, especially potentially toxic heavy metals, has driven scientific efforts to develop more accurate and targeted selective detection devices [12–15]. Metal ions such as mercury (Hg²⁺) and cadmium (Cd²⁺) pose significant risks to both human health and the environment, highlighting the need for the development of robust detection methods [16–19]. Traditional sensing technologies as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma/atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) among others, although effective, often face challenges such as high cost, complexity, and limited portability. In contrast, CQDs, with their strong fluorescence and ease of functionalization, offer a promising alternative for developing cost-effective and portable sensors for heavy metal detection [20,21]. By introducing heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen into the carbon surface, the electronic and optical properties of CQDs can be significantly altered, enhancing their selectivity and sensitivity toward specific analytes. These modifications can improve the interaction between CQDs and target metal ions, leading to changes in fluorescence upon binding. For instance, incorporating nitrogen-containing groups through functionalization can create strong binding sites for metal ions, enhancing the selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs as fluorescent sensors [22,23].

CQDs exhibit variations in their internal structure and the surface chemistry based on their synthesis methods, carbon source and surface modification [24]. They have gathered attention due to the ease with which their optical properties can be fine-tuning through surface modification and heteroatom doping. Doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron introduces new electronic states in CQDs, altering their absorption and emission properties [22].

This study explores the synthesis and characterization of CQDs functionalized with l-cysteine, ethylenediamine, and diethylenetriamine, with the aim of assessing their potential as selective sensors for metal ions in aqueous environments. Various analytical techniques, including UV–Vis and FT-IR spectroscopies and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were employed to systematically evaluate the structural and optical properties of the functionalized CQDs. In addition, computational studies were performed to provide deeper insights into the interaction mechanisms between the CQDs and metal ions, with a particular focus on selectivity for Hg²⁺ and Cd²⁺.

All chemicals used in this work were analytical grade and used without further purification. The reagents included: citric acid (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), ethylenediamine (EDA), l-cysteine, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate, potassium nitrate, lead nitrate, iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate, cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate, mercury (II) nitrate, aluminum (III) nitrate nonahydrate, poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (average Mn 700), 2,2-dimethoxy- 2-phenylacetophenon, all sourced from Fisher Chemicals. Deionized (DI) ultrapure water was used in all experiments. Hydrothermal synthesis was performed using a conventional stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave.

CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and various surface modifiers, which incorporate different heteroatoms (S, N) into CQDs surface. For the synthesis, a 2 % w/v CA aqueous solution was blended with the respective amount of surface modifiers indicated in Table 1 , which are relative to one gram of precursor CA used to generate L-CQDs (L-cysteine), E-CQDs (ethylenediamine), and D-CQDs (diethylenetriamine), as suggested by Pu et al. [25]. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The synthesized CQDs were collected, labeled according to the surface modifier, and stored under refrigeration.

The optical properties of CQDs were assessed through UV–Vis spectroscopy using a Diode Array Analytik Jena Specord S600 Spectrophotometer, and by Fluorescence spectroscopy using an Ocean Optics QEPro series High Sensitivity PL Spectrometer. Functional groups were identified using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FTIR Spectrometer. The structural features of CQDs were determined by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using a FEI microscope model Tecnai G2 Spirit Twin equipped with an Eagle 4k HR camera.

To evaluate ion sensitivity through fluorescence, 50 ppm solutions of Hg(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, and Fe(NO3)3 were used to observe the fluorescence quenching of CQDs in water. A 100 µL aliquot of 0.6 % v/v CQDs aqueous dispersion was poured into each of seven appropriately labeled test tubes, each corresponding to a different metal ion solution to be evaluated. Then, 3 mL of the respective metal ion solution was added to each test tube. One tube served as a control, containing only the CQDs solution. After a 30-minute incubation at room temperature, fluorescence spectra were recorded after 365, 465, and 635 nm excitation. Comparison of the fluorescence intensities obtained with the added ions versus the control without ions allowed for the evaluation of fluorescence quenching and the determination of the selectivity of the CQDs towards the different ions.

Computational studies on the interaction involved in the system of CQDs-modifier-metal ion, specifically for Hg2+, were conducted using ORCA 5.1 software. Four structures were created based on ordered graphene layered structures, incorporating different modifiers: NH2, OH, SH2, and one without modifiers. Then, once one Hg2+ion was incorporated, these structures were optimized to compare the different interaction it has with each selected heteroatom.

The Avogadro 1.2 tool was used to generate Orca input files. All compounds were optimized using the CAM-B3LYP functional with the def2-TZVP basis set and including D4 form of dispersion interaction. Once the structures have been optimized, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were calculated. For evaluating this aspect, TD-DFT has been used with def2-TZVP basis-set, D4 dispersion to calculate the first 30 excited states. For fluorescence analysis, the first excited state has been optimized through the introduction of an additional parameter IROOT in the TD-DFT section on the input, and the first 30 excited states have subsequently calculated.

Infrared spectroscopy was used to determine the functional groups present in CQDs surface. Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of functionalized samples. The comparison of L-CQDs, E-CQDs, and D-CQDs with pristine CQDs, reveals several characteristic bands, associated with the functional groups retained from their respective modifiers.

The surface modification of CQDs by different modifiers species was demonstrated by FTIR (Fig. 2, blue spectra). Some bands occurring in the CQDs spectra come from both the precursor CA (Fig. 2, grey spectra) and the modifiers (Fig. 2, green spectra). For L-CQDs, COH stretching vibrations are located at 3450 cm−1, a weak band around 2500–2000 cm−1 can be attributed to S-H stretching vibrations, suggesting potential surface modifications due to the thiol group. In the region of 1700–1600 cm−1, the L-CQDs exhibit bands that can be assigned to C=O stretching vibrations, which can be attributed to carboxyl and amide functional groups, this latter suggesting a chemical reaction between the functional groups of l-cysteine and pristine citric acid CQDs. The bands between 1400 and 1300 cm−1 are associated with CH and OH deformations. Additionally, the region of 1200–1000 cm−1 shows bands corresponding to CO and CN stretching vibrations. Notably, the bands in the region of 700–600 cm−1 are indicative of C-S stretching vibrations. Most of the FTIR features described before, are in agreement with the literature [26–29].

In the case of E-CQDs and D-CQDs the following bands were observed: COH stretching vibrations are located at 3450 cm−1, CH at 2950 cm −1 and 2850 cm −1, bending vibrations of NH at 1600 cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of C=O at 1650 cm−1. This latter band could be assigned not only to a carboxylic moiety but also the presence of an amide is possible, this analysis suggests that during the synthesis of CQDs from DETA and CA, chemical reactions have occurred, leading to the formation of new functionalities, mainly amides, which were not present in the original precursors [30,31]. In the case of D-CQDs, an extra band at 1126 cm−1 is assigned to asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC. This spectroscopic analysis is consistent with most of those observed in E-CQDs and D-CQDs [32–34].

The morphology and particle size of the modified CQDs, were determined by TEM. Representative images in Fig. 3 show that CQDs are well-dispersed and spherical, with no notable agglomeration. Particle diameters of these L-CQDs, E-CQDs, and D-CQDs range from 2 to 9 nm and the average size taken from several micrographs is around 5 nm, indicating that regardless of surface modifiers, the resulting CQDs exhibit a uniform size. This may suggest that the synthesis process is robust and controlled, consistently achieving particles of uniform size. The similarity in size can be important for applications where strict control of CQDs size is required [35].

The UV–Vis spectrum of L-CQDs is presented in Fig. 4 a. The spectrum exhibits a distinctive absorption peak in the 200–250 nm range, attributed to
π −
π
*
transitions of C=C and C=O bonds present in the CQDs. Beyond this peak, the absorbance decreases markedly, maintaining a low and relatively constant profile within the 300–600 nm range, except for a very small transition at 345 nm [36]. This spectral behavior suggests the absence of significant electronic transitions in the latter region, which is characteristic of CQDs lacking extensively conjugated functional groups [37]. The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of CQDs synthesized from EDA and Fig. 4b exhibits two distinct absorption peaks. The first peak, centered at approximately 270–280 nm, corresponds to
π −
π
*
transition of the conjugated carbon-carbon units associated with the carbon core [33]. A second, less intense peak in the 350–380 nm region suggests the presence of surface states or defects, likely originating from functional groups on the CQDs surface suggesting nonbonding orbital to antibonding pi orbital,
n −
π
*
transitions of the carbonyl/amine functional groups present on E-CQDs [38]. These spectral characteristics align with existing literature reports on CQDs synthesized from similar precursors. Additionally, these CQDs demonstrate strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation, confirming the successful synthesis of fluorescent E-CQDs. The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of D-CQDs synthesized is presented in Fig. 4c. The spectrum exhibits two distinct absorption peaks. The first peak, observed in the range of 200–250 nm, is attributed to
π −
π
*
transitions of C=C and C=O bonds present in the CQDs. The second peak, located in the 350–400 nm range, corresponds to
n −
π
*
transitions of C=O or C–OH bonds within the CQDs structure. Fluorescence spectroscopy reveals that these CQDs exhibit optimal emission in the blue region of the visible spectrum. Notably, neither the citric acid nor the diethylenetriamine precursor solutions display luminescence in the visible region when excited at the wavelength used for CQDs analysis. This observation confirms that the observed bright blue fluorescence originates exclusively from the synthesized CQDs, capable of absorbing the 365 nm radiation [39].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for CQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . Modified CQDs have optimal emission at excitation wavelengths of 365 nm and 470 nm for L-CQDs, E-CQDs and D-CQDs, however, under the excitation at 635 nm no photoluminescent activity is observed for any CQDs. In each Fig. 5-a,b,c, a fluorescence image of each CQDs dispersion after excitation at 365 nm, is displayed as an inset of figures. The dispersions exhibit a strong blue emission, which is consistent with the emission peak observed in the graph. This suggests that under these specific excitation conditions, the CQDs exhibit robust fluorescent properties, which is consistent with findings in related studies [27]. For L-CQDs, emission peaks are located at 494 nm under 365 nm excitation and 548 nm under 470 nm excitation, which is consistent with the expected red shift in the fluorescence phenomenon. In the spectra shown in Fig. 5b, carbon quantum dots synthesized with ethylenediamine, E-CQDs, showed distinct emission peaks under various excitation wavelengths. Notably, at 365 nm excitation, a prominent emission peak was observed at 476 nm. At 465 nm excitation, a significant emission peak appeared at 526 nm with less intensity, indicating excitation-dependent photoluminescence. In the literature, slightly different results are reported for E-CQDs synthesized by different routes, for example, microwave-assisted QDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine exhibit an emission peak at 454 nm at 360 nm of excitation [40]. In another study, it was demonstrated that fluorescent carbon dot from citric acid and ethylenediamine shows an emission maximum at 441 nm (the emission region is mainly concentrated at 400–550 nm) at the same excitation wavelength, however, for this last example the authors claim that the morphology their E-CQDs provides direct evidence that they are not real carbon dots since TEM images reveals particles with different size distribution ranging from nanometer to micrometer [32]. In the fluorescence emission spectrum, the D-CQDs exhibited distinct emission peaks under different excitation wavelengths. Specifically, under 365 nm excitation, a notable emission peak appeared at 426 nm. Under 470 nm excitation, a significant emission peak was observed at 531 nm with a lower intensity. According to the literature, graphene carbon dots with blue emission through reflux treatment of citric acid in the presence of diethylenetriamine demonstrates strong photoluminescence emission peak obtained at 461 nm when excited at 440 nm [41]. Fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopic properties are resumed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CQDs with concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
Knowing the fluorescence response of CQDs with concentration is important since we need that they hold their original characteristics allowing experiments and applications to be replicated. This experiment and the rest of the studies ahead were performed using the excitation 365 nm since the highest PL response was achieved with this excitation in all cases. Concentrations of CQDs were determined with a calibration curve using
n −
π
*
transition (Figure S1).

In Fig. 6 a, the behavior of the fluorescence emission of CQDs functionalized with l-cysteine with respect to their concentration is presented. The results show that fluorescence intensities increase as dilution of the CQDs increases from 0.108 % w/v (less diluted) to 0.014 % w/v (more diluted). In addition, as the concentration of CQDs decreases (from 0.108 % w/v to 0.014 % w/v), a red shift into longer wavelengths is observed (460 to 528 nm).

Fig. 6c, shows fluorescence spectra of CQDs functionalized with diethylenetriamine for several dilutions, from 0.085 % w/v (less diluted) to 0.011 % w/v (more diluted). As in the case of L-CQDs, a redshift towards longer wavelengths is observed. Finally, in the case of D-CQDs, a shift of the maximum emission wavelength from 460 nm to 480 nm is observed as the concentration of QDs decreases. PL intensity increasing with dilution, being higher at 0.085 % w/v (less diluted) to 0.011 % w/v (more diluted), suggesting that the reduction of self-absorption and quenching phenomena in more diluted solutions improves emission. This behavior is consistent with previous studies showing that concentration affects fluorescence intensity as a result of these mechanisms, highlighting the importance of concentration in the optical properties of D-CQDs.

In many fluorescent organic molecules, when they are aggregated or concentrated at high levels, their fluorescence decreases. This phenomenon is known as aggregation-induced quenching and is explained as follows: when these molecules are close enough, their planar polycyclic aromatic ring structure causes them to orient due to strong
π −
π
*
stacking intermolecular interactions. This overlap allows the photoexcited state to return to the ground state through a non-radiative transition, resulting in luminescence quenching [42]. The same effect has been commonly observed in CQDs [43]. This phenomenon contrasts with CQDs behavior in the monodisperse state, where the molecules are separated. In traditional luminophores, strong and planar intermolecular interactions in the aggregated state can lead to the formation of non-radiative excitonic states or facilitate non-radiative energy transfer between aggregated molecules. These interactions can cause the excited energy to dissipate as heat rather than being emitted as light, resulting in weakened or extinguished emission in the aggregated state [44].

On the other hand, Fig. 6b, shows fluorescence spectra of carbon quantum dots synthesized with ethylenediamine demonstrating a different behavior to that of diethylenetriamine and l-cystein. Unlike another CQDs, including L-CQDs and D-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity is higher in less diluted solutions (0.085 % w/v) and decreases as the solution becomes more diluted (0.011 % w/v). This behavior has been observed in CQDs systems exhibiting restrictive intramolecular motion (RIM) leading to a red shift aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect, [42,45,46], which refers to fluorophores emit weakly in very dilute solutions but radiate strongly in an aggregated form. Moreover, the red shift PL emission observed as the E-CQDs concentration increases suggests that aggregation process could affect the fluorescence response. As has been reported, AIE effect is a solvent polarity sensitive phenomenon with a better performance in nonpolar solvents [47]. In this work, distilled water was used as solvent, therefore, it would be expected to have a greater performance for lower polarity solvents [46–49].

The selectivity of the prepared L-CQDs, E-CQDs, and D-CQDs for specific metal ions was examined by mixing the CQDs with various ion solutions (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, K⁺, Cd²⁺, Co²⁺, Hg²⁺, and Pb²⁺), each at a concentration of 50 ppm.

Fig. 7 a illustrates that the fluorescent L-CQDs do not exhibit sensitivity towards a single ion but rather demonstrate a heterogeneous response across various metal cation species. The results reveal a pronounced response to Cd²⁺, followed by Al³⁺, Fe³⁺ and K⁺, while the sensitivity is notably lower for ions such as Co²⁺, Hg²⁺, and Pb²⁺. These findings indicate that the L-CQDs-based fluorescence sensor lacks high specificity for any individual cation but possesses the capability to detect multiple cationic species with varying degrees of sensitivity. By contrast, the evaluation of fluorescence quenching effects induced by various metal ions on E-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 7b, revealed a substantially greater quenching effect from Hg²⁺ compared to other ionic species. The fluorescence emission intensity of the E-CQDs decreased by >90 % upon exposure to the Hg²⁺ solution. As illustrated in Fig. 7c, the introduction of Hg²⁺ to diethylenetriamine-derived CQDs resulted in a significant fluorescence quenching effect (exceeding 90 %), whereas other metal ions induced only minimal quenching. These results demonstrate that D-CQDs exhibit excellent sensing capabilities, characterized by high selectivity for Hg²⁺ detection. Hg²⁺ exhibits a high affinity for nitrogen-based electron donors, resulting in robust interactions with the amine groups present in CQDs functionalized with ethylenediamine. Quenching effect of Cd2+ over L-CQDs PL, and quenching effect of Hg2+ over E-CQDs and D-CQDs PL are illustrated in Figs. 8 a, 8b, and 8c respectively. In addition, calibration curves for different concentrations of the target metal ions were established to demonstrate the detection capability of modified CQDs. In Fig. 8d, calibration curve for the detection of Cd2+ in a range of concentrations of 0.5 to 30 ppm by L-CQDs is shown, whereas in Figs. 8e and 8f, calibration curve for the detection of Hg2+ in a range of concentrations of 0.5 to 10 ppm by E-CQDs and D-CQDs respectively are shown. In all the cases, linear correlations are above 0.995, reaching detection limits of 0.36 ppm in the case of the system E-CQDs-Hg2+.

Theoretical studies on the interaction of CQDs-dopant-metal ion were conducted using ORCA 5.1 software. Four structures were created based on ordered graphene layered structures, incorporating different modifiers: NH2, OH, SH2, and one without modifiers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The geometry optimization of CQDs and CQDs with Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The representations of the CQDs without modifiers (C-QDs) and with N (NCQDs), O (OCQDs), and S (S-CQDs) modifiers can be observed in Fig. 9 , and with the incorporation of Hg2+.

Table 2 exhibits the distances between Hg atom and different types of functionalized models: C-QDs, N-QDs, O-QDs, and S-QDs. These results allow us to conclude that there is no coordination of the metal, but only weak interactions could be proposed. Therefore, it mostly follows the van der Waals radius, except for carbon C-QDs (4.946 Å) and S-QDs (3.24711 Å). With carbon, there is only a very weak interaction. The case of sulfur is interesting because a shorter distance with mercury is observed, suggesting a slightly stronger interaction.

In addition, the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) energies of the theoretical structures with and without the interaction with Hg2+ are presented in Table 3 . When comparing the structures before and after interaction with Hg²⁺, all show a decrease in ΔE. This suggests that the interaction with Hg²⁺ significantly affects the energy gap, possibly making the material more conductive or altering its optical properties. Experimental and theoretical results corroborate this idea. The sensitivity of a sensor, particularly one that operates through electron transfer, can be greatly modified by the HOMO-LUMO energy gap. The smaller the energy gap, the more easily electrons can be excited and transferred to an analyte, leading to high charge transfer from the analyte to the electrode, resulting in high detection sensitivity. Additionally, since less energy is required to excite and move the electrons, the sensor is likely to have a much faster response time. This ease of electronic excitation can provide greater sensitivity for detecting lower concentrations of the analyte.

However, sensitivity also depends on additional factors, such as the interaction energy. According to the interaction energy (Ei), presented in Table 4 , it is the energy required to form a complex between the CQDs and mercury (Hg2+). This energy can be calculated as the difference between the total energy of the model with Hg2+ and the individual energies of the separated components (CQDs and Hg2+).

The basic equation to calculate the interaction energy is:
E i =
E
s t r u c t u r e
H
g
2 +
−
(
E
s t r u c t u r e
+
E
H
g
2 +
)
E
s t r u c t u r e
H
g
2 +
:
Total energy of the structure with Hg.

E
s t r u c t u r e
: Total energy of the structure alone.

E
H
g
2 +
: Total energy of mercury alone.

From these results, a theoretical order of preferential interaction with Hg2+ can be constructed: E(C)<E(O)<E(S)<E(N). This suggests that introducing nitrogen or sulfur groups would always lead to a greater interaction with Hg2+ and should be thus present when thinking about the design of mercury detection.

The different structures with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen were derived from a carbon-only framework and optimized. Using the TDDFT/CAM-B3LYP-D4 method, the absorption spectra of these theoretical models were compared with the experimental spectra of L-CQDs (Fig. S2), E-CQDs (Fig. S3), and D-CQDs (Fig. S4).

The comparison reveals that nitrogen has the most significant impact on absorbance, followed by oxygen and sulfur. Theoretical structures align with experimental spectra, indicating that the 340 nm band in L-CQDs is primarily influenced by nitrogen, with contributions from oxygen and sulfur. In E-CQDs spectrum, nitrogen remains as the main absorbance contributor, particularly at the 341 nm band (Fig. S3). highlights a red-shifted absorption peak (358 nm) in D-CQDs, attributed to the presence of diethylenetriamine functional groups, such as amines, which significantly alter CQD absorption (Fig. S4). Simplified models with nitrogen and oxygen also exhibit UV absorption but are less red-shifted, underscoring the impact of more complex functional groups. Each functional group in carbon structures directly affects the optical properties of CQDs. The use of l-cysteine in CQD synthesis induces a shift in absorption, suggesting that the amino and thiol groups in l-cysteine interact with the carbon matrix to modify optical properties. Ethylenediamine, being smaller, does not cause as red-shifted absorption as diethylenetriamine, which interacts more effectively with the carbon matrix due to its larger size and more amino groups, resulting in significant absorption influence. In summary, the theoretical results are consistent with the experimental findings. Strategically, it is advantageous to prioritize nitrogen groups for improved absorption. While oxygen groups yield high absorption intensity, they exhibit lower energetic affinity. Conversely, sulfur groups display favorable affinity but lower absorption intensity. Thus, nitrogen groups appear to be the most effective for functionalizing carbon dots to increase their absorption properties.

The emission spectrum of CQDs synthesized with l-cysteine, ethylenediamine and diethylenetriamine and theoretical models are presented in Supplementary Material (Figs. S5-S7) . Experimental L-CQDs PL spectra and theoretical models with the respective functional groups exhibited blue-green emission peaks, revealing their contribution to different spectral regions. The emission states are induced or modified by the amino and thiol groups in l-cysteine. Similarly, E-CQDs compared with the theoretical models containing nitrogen, pure carbon, and oxygen show blue-green emission peaks, which are consistent with the experiment. The emission peaks in ethylenediamine CQDs can be attributed to electronic transitions due to amino groups, suggesting multiple emission centers or energy transfer effects in the CQDs. D-CQDs exhibit a broad emission spectrum covering blue to green emissions, possibly due to different amino groups in diethylenetriamine creating more emission states or altering existing ones. Theoretical models with nitrogen, pure carbon, and oxygen show emission maxima in the blue-green range, though they are less shifted and structured compared to experimental CQDs. The peaks in experimental spectra can be attributed to characteristic electronic transitions supported by amino groups. Nitrogen and oxygen exhibit more intense emission with similar behavior, while carbon displays lower emission intensity. Sulfur, although not as intense, has its emission shifted further to the right. Therefore, functionalizing with nitrogen is a good strategy, as it ensures strong energetic interaction, enhances UV absorption, and increases emission intensity. Additionally, incorporating sulfur can modulate the emission frequency (changing the color) due to its good interaction, although it slightly decreases fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, functionalization can improve the capture of specific ions, as seen with nitrogen, which enhances the detection of mercury ions.

This study investigates the conditions necessary to improve the selectivity of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) for detecting specific metal ions in water. Using l-cysteine, ethylenediamine, and diethylenetriamine as precursors, we successfully synthesized CQDs through an optimized one-step hydrothermal route obtaining well-dispersed and spherical particles with average sizes around 5 nm in all the cases, achieving that regardless of surface modifiers, the resulting CQDs exhibit a uniform size. Thus, our synthesis process is robust and controlled, allowing the direct comparison on the properties of the three different heteroatoms (N, S, O) doped CQDs. Optical properties of CQDs were performed through UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectroscopies, the results indicate that doping with heteroatoms significantly alters the absorption and emission properties of CQDs. Particularly, nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) enhance their PL response, making them ideal for sensor applications. Chemical structure and morphological analysis were determined by FTIR and TEM analysis respectively, FTIR results reveal the incorporation of heteroatom dopants inducing chemical changes in the surface of the dots. Morphological analysis reveals that the size of CQDs ranged from 3 to 5 nm and no agglomeration is presented using the optimized hydrothermal synthetic route. Concentration studies demonstrated that L-CQDs and D-CQDs suffer from aggregation-caused quenching at concentrations of 0.011 % w/v, while in E-CQDs, fluorescence intensity is higher in less diluted solution. Selectivity studies against various metal ions (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, K⁺, Cd²⁺, Co²⁺, Hg²⁺) showed that L-CQDs are sensitive to Cd²⁺, while E-CQDs and D-CQDs are highly selective for Hg²⁺. Theoretical models revealed small band gaps for the CQDs, making them ideal for sensor applications. Functional groups in the carbon structures significantly affect the optical properties of CQDs, with l-cysteine modifying absorption due to its amino and thiol groups, and diethylenetriamine causing a greater shift in absorption due to its larger size and greater number of amino groups. From a design perspective it can be inferred from theoretical models that for mercury detection N groups enhance greatly the interaction and absorption while S groups could be interesting to modulate the fluorescence color. This research highlights the importance of controlled synthesis and functionalization in enhancing the selectivity and optical properties of CQDs, providing valuable insights for developing advanced materials for environmental monitoring and the detection and mitigation of metal contamination in water resources.

María Belén Cánchig: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Floralba López: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zaillmar Morales-Navarro: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Alexis Debut: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Karla Vizuete: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Thibault Terencio: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manuel Caetano: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Juan Pablo Saucedo-Vázquez: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

Highly Luminescent Aggregate-Induced Emission from Polyethylene Glycol-Coated Carbon Quantum Dot Clusters under Blue Light Illumination. Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 2020, 8, 16569–16576. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0tc04007j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with aggregation induced emission enhancement for visual permittivity detection
Chemical Communications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. X. Liu, Z. L. Wu, M. X. Gao, H. Liu, and C. Z. Huang, “Carbon dots with aggregation induced emission enhancement for visual permittivity detection,” Chemical Communications, vol. 52, no. 10, pp. 2063–2066, 2016, doi: 10.1039/c5cc08635c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review—Aggregation-Induced Emission in Carbon Dots for Potential Applications
